Here, at last, he would expect to find a resolution of the puzzle. No doubt, he would say to himself, the existing Confederation must contain the most explicit provisions against military establishments in time of peace; and a departure from this model, on a point held dear, has occasioned the discontent that appears to drive these political champions.

If he were now to apply himself to a careful and critical reading of the Articles of Confederation, his astonishment would not only increase, but would take on a mixture of indignation, at the unexpected discovery that those articles, instead of containing the prohibition he had been looking for — and though they had, with jealous circumspection, restricted the authority of the state legislatures in this particular — had not imposed a single restraint on that of the United States. If he happened to be a man of quick sensibility or ardent temper, he could no longer hold back from regarding these outcries as the dishonest artifices of a sinister and unprincipled opposition to a plan that ought at least to receive a fair and candid examination from all sincere lovers of their country! How else, he would say, could the authors of these outcries have been tempted to vent such loud condemnations of that plan, on a point where it appears to have conformed itself to the general sense of America as declared in its various forms of government — and where it has even added a new and powerful safeguard unknown to any of them? If, on the contrary, he happened to be a man of calm and dispassionate feelings, he would allow himself a sigh for the frailty of human nature, and would lament that in a matter so important to the happiness of millions, the true merits of the question should be muddled and entangled by maneuvers so hostile to an impartial and sound determination. Even such a man could hardly resist observing that conduct of this kind bears too much the appearance of an intention to mislead the people by inflaming their passions, rather than to persuade them by arguments addressed to their understanding.

But however little support this objection finds, even in precedents from our own experience, it may be worthwhile to take a closer look at its intrinsic merits. A careful examination will show that restraints on the legislature's discretion regarding military establishments in peacetime would be improper to impose, and that even if imposed, the necessities of society would make them unlikely to be observed.

Though a wide ocean separates the United States from Europe, there are nonetheless various considerations that warn us against excessive confidence or a false sense of security. On one side of us, stretching far into our rear, are growing settlements subject to the dominion of Britain. On the other side, extending to meet the British settlements, are colonies and establishments subject to the dominion of Spain. This situation, together with the proximity of the West India Islands belonging to these two powers, creates between them — with respect to their American possessions and in relation to us — a common interest. The savage tribes on our Western frontier ought to be regarded as our natural enemies, their natural allies, because they have the most to fear from us and the most to hope from them. The advances in the art of navigation have, as to the ease of communication, rendered distant nations largely neighbors to one another. Britain and Spain are among the principal maritime powers of Europe. A future alignment of interests between these nations ought not to be regarded as improbable. The increasing distance of kinship is every day weakening the force of the family compact between France and Spain. And statesmen have always had strong reason to regard the ties of blood as weak and unreliable links of political connection. These circumstances combined warn us not to be too

optimistic in thinking ourselves entirely out of reach of danger.

Before the Revolution, and ever since the peace, there has been a constant need to maintain small garrisons on our Western frontier. No one can doubt that these will continue to be indispensable, if only to guard against the raids and depredations of the Indians. These garrisons must either be supplied by occasional detachments drawn from the militia, or by permanent corps in the pay of the government. The first option is impracticable; and even if it were practicable, it would be harmful. The militia would not long — if at all — submit to being dragged from their occupations and families to perform that most disagreeable duty in times of profound peace. And if they could be persuaded or compelled to do it, the increased expense of a frequent rotation of service, and the loss of labor and disruption of the productive pursuits of individuals, would constitute conclusive objections to the scheme. It would be as burdensome and injurious to the public as it would be ruinous to private citizens. The latter resource — permanent corps in the pay of the government — amounts to a standing army in time of peace; a small one, indeed, but not the less real for being small. Here is a straightforward view of the subject, that shows us at once the impropriety of a constitutional ban on such establishments, and the necessity of leaving the matter to the discretion and prudence of the legislature.

As our strength grows, it is probable — indeed, it may be said certain — that Britain and Spain would expand their military presence in our neighborhood. If we were unwilling to be left in a naked and defenseless condition and subject to their insults and encroachments, we would find it expedient to increase our frontier garrisons in some proportion to the force by which our Western settlements might be threatened. There are, and will be, particular posts whose control commands large stretches of territory and makes future invasions of the rest easier. It may be added that some of those posts will be keys to trade with the Indian nations. Can anyone think it would be wise to leave such posts in a condition where they could be seized at any moment by one or the other of two neighboring and formidable powers? To act this way would be to abandon all the usual standards of prudence and sound policy.

If we intend to be a commercial nation, or even to be secure on our Atlantic coast, we must work, as soon as possible, to build a navy. To that end, there must be shipyards and arsenals; and for the defense of these, fortifications, and probably garrisons. When a nation has grown so powerful at sea that it can protect its shipyards with its fleets, this eliminates the need for garrisons for that purpose; but where naval establishments are in their infancy, moderate garrisons will, in all likelihood, prove to be an indispensable safeguard against raids aimed at destroying the arsenals and shipyards, and sometimes the fleet itself.

publication.raw_dateline: For the Independent Journal. Wednesday, December 19, 1787

items.title: The Same Subject Continued (The Powers Necessary to the Common Defense Further Considered)
paragraphs: To the People of the State of New York:

IT MAY perhaps be urged that the objects enumerated in the preceding number ought to be provided for by the State governments, under the direction of the Union. But this would be, in reality, an inversion of the primary principle of our political association, as it would in practice transfer the care of the common defense from the federal head to the individual members: a project oppressive to some States, dangerous to all, and baneful to the Confederacy.

The territories of Britain, Spain, and of the Indian nations in our neighborhood do not border on particular States, but encircle the Union from Maine to Georgia. The danger, though in different degrees, is therefore common. And the means of guarding against it ought, in like manner, to be the objects of common councils and of a common treasury. It happens that some States, from local situation, are more directly exposed. New York is of this class. Upon the plan of separate provisions, New York would have to sustain the whole weight of the establishments requisite to her immediate safety, and to the mediate or ultimate protection of her neighbors. This would neither be equitable as it respected New York nor safe as it respected the other States. Various inconveniences would attend such a system. The States, to whose lot it might fall to support the necessary establishments, would be as little able as willing, for a considerable time to come, to bear the burden of competent provisions. The security of all would thus be subjected to the parsimony, improvidence, or inability of a part. If the resources of such part becoming more abundant and extensive, its provisions should be proportionally enlarged, the other States would quickly take the alarm at seeing the whole military force of the Union in the hands of two or three of its members, and those probably amongst the most powerful. They would each choose to have some counterpoise, and pretenses could easily be contrived. In this situation, military establishments, nourished by mutual jealousy, would be apt to swell beyond their natural or proper size; and being at the separate disposal of the members, they would be engines for the abridgment or demolition of the national authority.

Reasons have been already given to induce a supposition that the State governments will too naturally be prone to a rivalship with that of the Union, the foundation of which will be the love of power; and that in any contest between the federal head and one of its members the people will be most apt to unite with their local government. If, in addition to this immense advantage, the ambition of the members should be stimulated by the separate and independent possession of military forces, it would afford too strong a temptation and too great a facility to them to make enterprises upon, and finally to subvert, the constitutional authority of the Union. On the other hand, the liberty of the people would be less safe in this state of things than in that which left the national forces in the hands of the national government. As far as an army may be considered as a dangerous weapon of power, it had better be in those hands of which the people are most likely to be jealous than in those of which they are least likely to be jealous. For it is a truth, which the experience of ages has attested, that the people are always most in danger when the means of injuring their rights are in the possession of those of whom they entertain the least suspicion.

The framers of the existing Confederation, fully aware of the danger to the Union from the separate possession of military forces by the States, have, in express terms, prohibited them from having either ships or troops, unless with the consent of Congress. The truth is, that the existence of a federal government and military establishments under State authority are not less at variance with each other than a due supply of the federal treasury and the system of quotas and requisitions.

There are other lights besides those already taken notice of, in which the impropriety of restraints on the discretion of the national legislature will be equally manifest. The design of the objection, which has been mentioned, is to preclude standing armies in time of peace, though we have never been informed how far it is designed the prohibition should extend; whether to raising armies as well as to KEEPING THEM UP in a season of tranquillity or not. If it be confined to the latter it will have no precise signification, and it will be ineffectual for the purpose intended. When armies are once raised what shall be denominated "keeping them up," contrary to the sense of the Constitution? What time shall be requisite to ascertain the violation? Shall it be a week, a month, a year? Or shall we say they may be continued as long as the danger which occasioned their being raised continues? This would be to admit that they might be kept up IN TIME OF PEACE, against threatening or impending danger, which would be at once to deviate from the literal meaning of the prohibition, and to introduce an extensive latitude of construction. Who shall judge of the continuance of the danger? This must undoubtedly be submitted to the national government, and the matter would then be brought to this issue, that the national government, to provide against apprehended danger, might in the first instance raise troops, and might afterwards keep them on foot as long as they supposed the peace or safety of the community was in any degree of jeopardy. It is easy to perceive that a discretion so latitudinary as this would afford ample room for eluding the force of the provision.

The supposed utility of a provision of this kind can only be founded on the supposed probability, or at least possibility, of a combination between the executive and the legislative, in some scheme of usurpation. Should this at any time happen, how easy would it be to fabricate pretenses of approaching danger! Indian hostilities, instigated by Spain or Britain, would always be at hand. Provocations to produce the desired appearances might even be given to some foreign power, and appeased again by timely concessions. If we can reasonably presume such a combination to have been formed, and that the enterprise is warranted by a sufficient prospect of success, the army, when once raised, from whatever cause, or on whatever pretext, may be applied to the execution of the project.

If, to obviate this consequence, it should be resolved to extend the prohibition to the RAISING of armies in time of peace, the United States would then exhibit the most extraordinary spectacle which the world has yet seen, that of a nation incapacitated by its Constitution to prepare for defense, before it was actually invaded. As the ceremony of a formal denunciation of war has of late fallen into disuse, the presence of an enemy within our territories must be waited for, as the legal warrant to the government to begin its levies of men for the protection of the State. We must receive the blow, before we could even prepare to return it. All that kind of policy by which nations anticipate distant danger, and meet the gathering storm, must be abstained from, as contrary to the genuine maxims of a free government. We must expose our property and liberty to the mercy of foreign invaders, and invite them by our weakness to seize the naked and defenseless prey, because we are afraid that rulers, created by our choice, dependent on our will, might endanger that liberty, by an abuse of the means necessary to its preservation.

Here I expect we shall be told that the militia of the country is its natural bulwark, and would be at all times equal to the national defense. This doctrine, in substance, had like to have lost us our independence. It cost millions to the United States that might have been saved. The facts which, from our own experience, forbid a reliance of this kind, are too recent to permit us to be the dupes of such a suggestion. The steady operations of war against a regular and disciplined army can only be successfully conducted by a force of the same kind. Considerations of economy, not less than of stability and vigor, confirm this position. The American militia, in the course of the late war, have, by their valor on numerous occasions, erected eternal monuments to their fame; but the bravest of them feel and know that the liberty of their country could not have been established by their efforts alone, however great and valuable they were. War, like most other things, is a science to be acquired and perfected by diligence, by perseverance, by time, and by practice.

All violent policy, as it is contrary to the natural and experienced course of human affairs, defeats itself. Pennsylvania, at this instant, affords an example of the truth of this remark. The Bill of Rights of that State declares that standing armies are dangerous to liberty, and ought not to be kept up in time of peace. Pennsylvania, nevertheless, in a time of profound peace, from the existence of partial disorders in one or two of her counties, has resolved to raise a body of troops; and in all probability will keep them up as long as there is any appearance of danger to the public peace. The conduct of Massachusetts affords a lesson on the same subject, though on different ground. That State (without waiting for the sanction of Congress, as the articles of the Confederation require) was compelled to raise troops to quell a domestic insurrection, and still keeps a corps in pay to prevent a revival of the spirit of revolt. The particular constitution of Massachusetts opposed no obstacle to the measure; but the instance is still of use to instruct us that cases are likely to occur under our government, as well as under those of other nations, which will sometimes render a military force in time of peace essential to the security of the society, and that it is therefore improper in this respect to control the legislative discretion. It also teaches us, in its application to the United States, how little the rights of a feeble government are likely to be respected, even by its own constituents. And it teaches us, in addition to the rest, how unequal parchment provisions are to a struggle with public necessity.

It was a fundamental maxim of the Lacedaemonian commonwealth, that the post of admiral should not be conferred twice on the same person. The Peloponnesian confederates, having suffered a severe defeat at sea from the Athenians, demanded Lysander, who had before served with success in that capacity, to command the combined fleets. The Lacedaemonians, to gratify their allies, and yet preserve the semblance of an adherence to their ancient institutions, had recourse to the flimsy subterfuge of investing Lysander with the real power of admiral, under the nominal title of vice-admiral. This instance is selected from among a multitude that might be cited to confirm the truth already advanced and illustrated by domestic examples; which is, that nations pay little regard to rules and maxims calculated in their very nature to run counter to the necessities of society. Wise politicians will be cautious about fettering the government with restrictions that cannot be observed, because they know that every breach of the fundamental laws, though dictated by necessity, impairs that sacred reverence which ought to be maintained in the breast of rulers towards the constitution of a country, and forms a precedent for other breaches where the same plea of necessity does not exist at all, or is less urgent and palpable.
plain_english: To the People of the State of New York:

It may perhaps be argued that the items enumerated in the preceding paper ought to be provided for by the state governments, under the direction of the Union. But this would be, in reality, an inversion of the foundational principle of our political association, as it would in practice transfer responsibility for the common defense from the federal head to the individual members: a project oppressive to some states, dangerous to all, and destructive to the Confederacy.

The territories of Britain, Spain, and of the Indian nations in our vicinity do not border on particular States, but encircle the Union from Maine to Georgia. The danger, though in different degrees, is therefore common. And the means of guarding against it ought, in like manner, to be the objects of common deliberation and a common treasury. It happens that some States, by virtue of their location, are more directly exposed. New York is of this class. Under a system of separate provisions, New York would have to bear the entire weight of the military establishments required for its own immediate safety and for the indirect or ultimate protection of its neighbors. This would be neither equitable as it concerned New York nor safe as it concerned the other States. Various problems would attend such a system. The States on which the burden of maintaining the necessary establishments happened to fall would be as little able as they were willing, for a considerable time to come, to bear the weight of adequate provisions. The security of all would thus be made dependent on the frugality , shortsightedness , or incapacity of a part. If the resources of that part were to grow more abundant and extensive, and its military provisions were enlarged proportionally, the other States would quickly take alarm at seeing the whole military force of the Union in the hands of two or three of its members — and those probably among the most powerful. Each would choose to have some counterweight, and pretexts could easily be manufactured. In this situation, military establishments, fed by mutual jealousy, would be prone to swell beyond their natural or proper size; and being at the separate disposal of individual members, they would become instruments for the curtailment or destruction of national authority.

Reasons have already been given to support the inference that state governments will be all too naturally inclined toward rivalry with the federal government, driven by the love of power, and that in any contest between the federal head and one of its member states, the people will be most likely to side with their local government. If, on top of this enormous advantage, the ambitions of the member states were further inflamed by the separate and independent possession of military forces, it would offer them too strong a temptation and too great a means to make moves against , and ultimately to destroy, the constitutional authority of the Union. On the other hand, the liberty of the people would be less safe under these circumstances than under an arrangement that left the national military forces in the hands of the national government. Insofar as an army may be considered a dangerous instrument of power, it is better placed in the hands of those whom the people are most likely to watch with suspicion than in the hands of those whom they are least likely to watch with suspicion. For it is a truth that the experience of ages has confirmed: the people are always in the greatest danger when the means of injuring their rights are held by those whom they suspect the least.

The framers of the existing Confederation, fully aware of the danger to the Union posed by the states' separate possession of military forces, expressly prohibited the states from maintaining either ships or troops without the consent of Congress. The truth is that the coexistence of a federal government and military establishments under state authority are no less incompatible with each other than an adequate supply of the federal treasury and the system of quotas and requisitions.

There are other angles, beyond those already examined, from which the impropriety of placing restrictions on the discretion of the national legislature will be equally clear. The purpose of the objection that has been raised is to prevent standing armies in time of peace — though we have never been told how far the prohibition is meant to reach: whether it extends to raising armies as well as to KEEPING THEM UP in a season of tranquility, or not. If it is confined to the latter, it will have no precise meaning, and it will be ineffective for the purpose intended. Once armies are raised, what exactly would constitute "keeping them up" in violation of the Constitution? How much time would have to pass before the violation could be established? Would it be a week, a month, a year? Or would we say that armies may be continued as long as the danger that caused them to be raised persists? That would be to concede that they might be kept up IN TIME OF PEACE against threatening or approaching danger — which would at once depart from the literal meaning of the prohibition and introduce an expansive latitude of construction. Who is to judge whether the danger continues? That question would inevitably fall to the national government, and the matter would then come down to this: that the national government, in order to guard against anticipated danger, might in the first instance raise troops, and might afterward keep them in the field for as long as it judged the peace or safety of the community to be in any degree at risk. It is easy to see that a discretion this broad would provide ample room for evading the force of the provision.

The supposed usefulness of a provision of this kind can only rest on the supposed probability, or at least possibility, of a conspiracy between the executive and the legislature in some scheme of usurpation. Should this ever happen, how easy would it be to manufacture pretexts of approaching danger! Indian hostilities, instigated by Spain or Britain, would always be ready at hand. Provocations designed to produce the desired appearances might even be given to some foreign power, and then smoothed over by timely concessions. If we can reasonably presume that such a conspiracy has been formed, and that the undertaking is supported by a sufficient prospect of success, the army — once raised, from whatever cause or on whatever pretext — can be turned to the execution of the project.

If, to avoid this consequence, it were resolved to extend the prohibition to the RAISING of armies in time of peace, the United States would then present the most extraordinary spectacle the world has yet seen: a nation rendered incapable by its own Constitution of preparing for defense before it was actually invaded. Since the formality of a formal declaration of war has lately fallen into disuse, the presence of an enemy within our territory would have to be waited for as the legal warrant authorizing the government to begin its levies of men for the protection of the State. We would have to receive the blow before we could even prepare to return it. Every form of policy by which nations anticipate distant danger and meet the gathering storm would have to be abandoned as contrary to the true principles of a free government. We would have to expose our property and liberty to the mercy of foreign invaders, and invite them by our weakness to seize a naked and defenseless prey — because we are afraid that rulers created by our own choice, dependent on our own will, might endanger that liberty by abusing the very means necessary to preserve it.

Here I expect we'll be told that the country's militia is its natural bulwark , and would at all times be equal to the task of national defense. This doctrine, in substance, nearly cost us our independence. It cost the United States millions that might have been saved. The facts that, from our own experience, argue against relying on this — facts too recent to allow us to be taken in by such a suggestion — forbid it entirely. The sustained operations of war against a regular and disciplined army can only be successfully conducted by a force of the same kind. Considerations of economy, no less than of stability and vigor, confirm this. The American militia, over the course of the recent war, erected by their valor on numerous occasions eternal monuments to their fame ; but the bravest of them feel and know that the liberty of their country could not have been secured by their efforts alone, however great and valuable those efforts were. War, like most other things, is a science to be acquired and perfected by diligence, by perseverance, by time, and by practice.

All violent policy, being contrary to the natural and well-documented course of human affairs, defeats itself. Pennsylvania, at this very moment, provides an example of this truth. The Bill of Rights of that state declares that standing armies are dangerous to liberty and ought not to be maintained in time of peace. Pennsylvania, nevertheless, in a time of profound peace — prompted by the existence of limited disorders in one or two of her counties — has resolved to raise a body of troops; and will in all probability keep them up as long as any threat to public peace remains visible. The conduct of Massachusetts offers a lesson on the same subject, though on different grounds. That state — without waiting for the authorization of Congress, as the Articles of Confederation require — was compelled to raise troops to put down a domestic insurrection, and still keeps a corps on payroll to prevent a revival of the spirit of revolt. The particular constitution of Massachusetts posed no obstacle to that measure; but the instance is still useful in instructing us that cases are likely to arise under our government, as under those of other nations, which will sometimes make a military force in time of peace essential to the security of society — and that it is therefore improper, in this respect, to constrain legislative discretion. It also teaches us, in its application to the United States, how little the rights of a weak government are likely to be respected, even by its own constituents. And it teaches us, beyond all of this, how unequal written provisions are to a struggle with public necessity.

It was a foundational principle of the Spartan republic that the office of admiral should not be given to the same person twice. The Peloponnesian allies, having suffered a crushing defeat at sea at the hands of the Athenians, demanded Lysander — who had previously served with success in that role — to command the combined fleets. The Spartans, in order to satisfy their allies while still keeping up the appearance of adherence to their ancient institutions, resorted to the flimsy subterfuge of investing Lysander with the real power of admiral under the nominal title of vice-admiral. This example is chosen from among a great many that could be cited to confirm the truth already established and illustrated by domestic examples: that nations pay little attention to rules and principles that are by their very nature destined to run up against the necessities of society. Wise statesmen will be careful about burdening the government with restrictions that cannot be observed, because they understand that every violation of the fundamental laws — even when compelled by necessity — erodes that sacred reverence which ought to be preserved in the minds of rulers toward the constitution of a country, and establishes a precedent for further violations in cases where the same claim of necessity does not exist at all, or is less urgent and plain.

publication.raw_dateline: From the New York Packet. Friday, December 21, 1787.

items.title: The Idea of Restraining the Legislative Authority in Regard to the Common Defense Considered.
paragraphs: To the People of the State of New York:

IT WAS a thing hardly to be expected that in a popular revolution the minds of men should stop at that happy mean which marks the salutary boundary between POWER and PRIVILEGE, and combines the energy of government with the security of private rights. A failure in this delicate and important point is the great source of the inconveniences we experience, and if we are not cautious to avoid a repetition of the error, in our future attempts to rectify and ameliorate our system, we may travel from one chimerical project to another; we may try change after change; but we shall never be likely to make any material change for the better.

The idea of restraining the legislative authority, in the means of providing for the national defense, is one of those refinements which owe their origin to a zeal for liberty more ardent than enlightened. We have seen, however, that it has not had thus far an extensive prevalency; that even in this country, where it made its first appearance, Pennsylvania and North Carolina are the only two States by which it has been in any degree patronized; and that all the others have refused to give it the least countenance; wisely judging that confidence must be placed somewhere; that the necessity of doing it, is implied in the very act of delegating power; and that it is better to hazard the abuse of that confidence than to embarrass the government and endanger the public safety by impolitic restrictions on the legislative authority. The opponents of the proposed Constitution combat, in this respect, the general decision of America; and instead of being taught by experience the propriety of correcting any extremes into which we may have heretofore run, they appear disposed to conduct us into others still more dangerous, and more extravagant. As if the tone of government had been found too high, or too rigid, the doctrines they teach are calculated to induce us to depress or to relax it, by expedients which, upon other occasions, have been condemned or forborne. It may be affirmed without the imputation of invective, that if the principles they inculcate, on various points, could so far obtain as to become the popular creed, they would utterly unfit the people of this country for any species of government whatever. But a danger of this kind is not to be apprehended. The citizens of America have too much discernment to be argued into anarchy. And I am much mistaken, if experience has not wrought a deep and solemn conviction in the public mind, that greater energy of government is essential to the welfare and prosperity of the community.

It may not be amiss in this place concisely to remark the origin and progress of the idea, which aims at the exclusion of military establishments in time of peace. Though in speculative minds it may arise from a contemplation of the nature and tendency of such institutions, fortified by the events that have happened in other ages and countries, yet as a national sentiment, it must be traced to those habits of thinking which we derive from the nation from whom the inhabitants of these States have in general sprung.

In England, for a long time after the Norman Conquest, the authority of the monarch was almost unlimited. Inroads were gradually made upon the prerogative, in favor of liberty, first by the barons, and afterwards by the people, till the greatest part of its most formidable pretensions became extinct. But it was not till the revolution in 1688, which elevated the Prince of Orange to the throne of Great Britain, that English liberty was completely triumphant. As incident to the undefined power of making war, an acknowledged prerogative of the crown, Charles II. had, by his own authority, kept on foot in time of peace a body of 5,000 regular troops. And this number James II. increased to 30,000; who were paid out of his civil list. At the revolution, to abolish the exercise of so dangerous an authority, it became an article of the Bill of Rights then framed, that "the raising or keeping a standing army within the kingdom in time of peace, UNLESS WITH THE CONSENT OF PARLIAMENT, was against law."

In that kingdom, when the pulse of liberty was at its highest pitch, no security against the danger of standing armies was thought requisite, beyond a prohibition of their being raised or kept up by the mere authority of the executive magistrate. The patriots, who effected that memorable revolution, were too temperate, too wellinformed, to think of any restraint on the legislative discretion. They were aware that a certain number of troops for guards and garrisons were indispensable; that no precise bounds could be set to the national exigencies; that a power equal to every possible contingency must exist somewhere in the government: and that when they referred the exercise of that power to the judgment of the legislature, they had arrived at the ultimate point of precaution which was reconcilable with the safety of the community.

From the same source, the people of America may be said to have derived an hereditary impression of danger to liberty, from standing armies in time of peace. The circumstances of a revolution quickened the public sensibility on every point connected with the security of popular rights, and in some instances raise the warmth of our zeal beyond the degree which consisted with the due temperature of the body politic. The attempts of two of the States to restrict the authority of the legislature in the article of military establishments, are of the number of these instances. The principles which had taught us to be jealous of the power of an hereditary monarch were by an injudicious excess extended to the representatives of the people in their popular assemblies. Even in some of the States, where this error was not adopted, we find unnecessary declarations that standing armies ought not to be kept up, in time of peace, WITHOUT THE CONSENT OF THE LEGISLATURE. I call them unnecessary, because the reason which had introduced a similar provision into the English Bill of Rights is not applicable to any of the State constitutions. The power of raising armies at all, under those constitutions, can by no construction be deemed to reside anywhere else, than in the legislatures themselves; and it was superfluous, if not absurd, to declare that a matter should not be done without the consent of a body, which alone had the power of doing it. Accordingly, in some of these constitutions, and among others, in that of this State of New York, which has been justly celebrated, both in Europe and America, as one of the best of the forms of government established in this country, there is a total silence upon the subject.

It is remarkable, that even in the two States which seem to have meditated an interdiction of military establishments in time of peace, the mode of expression made use of is rather cautionary than prohibitory. It is not said, that standing armies SHALL NOT BE kept up, but that they OUGHT NOT to be kept up, in time of peace. This ambiguity of terms appears to have been the result of a conflict between jealousy and conviction; between the desire of excluding such establishments at all events, and the persuasion that an absolute exclusion would be unwise and unsafe.

Can it be doubted that such a provision, whenever the situation of public affairs was understood to require a departure from it, would be interpreted by the legislature into a mere admonition, and would be made to yield to the necessities or supposed necessities of the State? Let the fact already mentioned, with respect to Pennsylvania, decide. What then (it may be asked) is the use of such a provision, if it cease to operate the moment there is an inclination to disregard it?

Let us examine whether there be any comparison, in point of efficacy, between the provision alluded to and that which is contained in the new Constitution, for restraining the appropriations of money for military purposes to the period of two years. The former, by aiming at too much, is calculated to effect nothing; the latter, by steering clear of an imprudent extreme, and by being perfectly compatible with a proper provision for the exigencies of the nation, will have a salutary and powerful operation.

The legislature of the United States will be OBLIGED, by this provision, once at least in every two years, to deliberate upon the propriety of keeping a military force on foot; to come to a new resolution on the point; and to declare their sense of the matter, by a formal vote in the face of their constituents. They are not AT LIBERTY to vest in the executive department permanent funds for the support of an army, if they were even incautious enough to be willing to repose in it so improper a confidence. As the spirit of party, in different degrees, must be expected to infect all political bodies, there will be, no doubt, persons in the national legislature willing enough to arraign the measures and criminate the views of the majority. The provision for the support of a military force will always be a favorable topic for declamation. As often as the question comes forward, the public attention will be roused and attracted to the subject, by the party in opposition; and if the majority should be really disposed to exceed the proper limits, the community will be warned of the danger, and will have an opportunity of taking measures to guard against it. Independent of parties in the national legislature itself, as often as the period of discussion arrived, the State legislatures, who will always be not only vigilant but suspicious and jealous guardians of the rights of the citizens against encroachments from the federal government, will constantly have their attention awake to the conduct of the national rulers, and will be ready enough, if any thing improper appears, to sound the alarm to the people, and not only to be the VOICE, but, if necessary, the ARM of their discontent.

Schemes to subvert the liberties of a great community REQUIRE TIME to mature them for execution. An army, so large as seriously to menace those liberties, could only be formed by progressive augmentations; which would suppose, not merely a temporary combination between the legislature and executive, but a continued conspiracy for a series of time. Is it probable that such a combination would exist at all? Is it probable that it would be persevered in, and transmitted along through all the successive variations in a representative body, which biennial elections would naturally produce in both houses? Is it presumable, that every man, the instant he took his seat in the national Senate or House of Representatives, would commence a traitor to his constituents and to his country? Can it be supposed that there would not be found one man, discerning enough to detect so atrocious a conspiracy, or bold or honest enough to apprise his constituents of their danger? If such presumptions can fairly be made, there ought at once to be an end of all delegated authority. The people should resolve to recall all the powers they have heretofore parted with out of their own hands, and to divide themselves into as many States as there are counties, in order that they may be able to manage their own concerns in person.

If such suppositions could even be reasonably made, still the concealment of the design, for any duration, would be impracticable. It would be announced, by the very circumstance of augmenting the army to so great an extent in time of profound peace. What colorable reason could be assigned, in a country so situated, for such vast augmentations of the military force? It is impossible that the people could be long deceived; and the destruction of the project, and of the projectors, would quickly follow the discovery.

It has been said that the provision which limits the appropriation of money for the support of an army to the period of two years would be unavailing, because the Executive, when once possessed of a force large enough to awe the people into submission, would find resources in that very force sufficient to enable him to dispense with supplies from the acts of the legislature. But the question again recurs, upon what pretense could he be put in possession of a force of that magnitude in time of peace? If we suppose it to have been created in consequence of some domestic insurrection or foreign war, then it becomes a case not within the principles of the objection; for this is levelled against the power of keeping up troops in time of peace. Few persons will be so visionary as seriously to contend that military forces ought not to be raised to quell a rebellion or resist an invasion; and if the defense of the community under such circumstances should make it necessary to have an army so numerous as to hazard its liberty, this is one of those calamities for which there is neither preventative nor cure. It cannot be provided against by any possible form of government; it might even result from a simple league offensive and defensive, if it should ever be necessary for the confederates or allies to form an army for common defense.

But it is an evil infinitely less likely to attend us in a united than in a disunited state; nay, it may be safely asserted that it is an evil altogether unlikely to attend us in the latter situation. It is not easy to conceive a possibility that dangers so formidable can assail the whole Union, as to demand a force considerable enough to place our liberties in the least jeopardy, especially if we take into our view the aid to be derived from the militia, which ought always to be counted upon as a valuable and powerful auxiliary. But in a state of disunion (as has been fully shown in another place), the contrary of this supposition would become not only probable, but almost unavoidable.
plain_english: To the People of the State of New York:

It was hardly to be expected that in a popular revolution the minds of men would stop at that happy medium which marks the healthy boundary between POWER and PRIVILEGE, and combines the energy of government with the security of private rights. A failure at this delicate and important point is the great source of the problems we experience, and if we are not careful to avoid repeating the error in our future attempts to correct and improve our system, we may travel from one fantastical scheme to another; we may try change after change; but we shall never be likely to make any material change for the better.

The idea of restraining the legislature's authority in the means of providing for national defense is one of those refinements that owe their origin to a zeal for liberty more ardent than enlightened. We have seen, however, that this idea has not so far achieved any widespread acceptance; that even in this country, where it first appeared, Pennsylvania and North Carolina are the only two states that have embraced it to any degree; and that all the others have refused to give it the slightest encouragement — wisely judging that confidence must be placed somewhere; that the necessity of doing so is implied in the very act of delegating power; and that it is better to risk the abuse of that confidence than to hamper the government and endanger public safety through ill-conceived restrictions on legislative authority. The opponents of the proposed Constitution are, in this respect, fighting against the general judgment of America; and instead of being taught by experience the wisdom of correcting any extremes into which we may previously have fallen, they appear inclined to lead us into others still more dangerous and more extravagant. As if the tone of government had been found too high, or too rigid, the doctrines they teach are calculated to lead us to suppress or weaken it, by means which, on other occasions, have been condemned or avoided. It may be stated without any charge of name-calling that if the principles they instill, on various points, were to gain sufficient ground to become the popular creed, they would make the people of this country wholly unfit for any form of government whatsoever. But a danger of this kind is not to be feared. The citizens of America have too much good judgment to be argued into anarchy. And I am much mistaken if experience has not produced a deep and solemn conviction in the public mind that greater energy of government is essential to the welfare and prosperity of the community.

It may not be out of place here to briefly note the origin and development of the idea that aims at excluding standing military forces in times of peace. Though in speculative minds it may arise from a direct contemplation of the nature and tendency of such institutions, reinforced by the events that have occurred in other ages and countries, yet as a national sentiment it must be traced back to those habits of thinking that we inherit from the nation from which the inhabitants of these States have generally descended.

In England, for a long time after the Norman Conquest, the authority of the monarch was almost unlimited. Inroads were gradually made upon the prerogative , in favor of liberty, first by the barons, and afterwards by the people, until the greatest part of its most formidable pretensions became extinct. But it was not until the revolution of 1688, which elevated the Prince of Orange to the throne of Great Britain, that English liberty was completely triumphant. As an incident of the undefined power of making war — an acknowledged prerogative of the Crown — Charles II had, by his own authority, kept on foot in time of peace a body of 5,000 regular troops. And this number James II increased to 30,000, who were paid out of his civil list . At the revolution, to abolish the exercise of so dangerous an authority, it became an article of the Bill of Rights then framed, that "the raising or keeping a standing army within the kingdom in time of peace, UNLESS WITH THE CONSENT OF PARLIAMENT, was against law."

In that kingdom, at the moment when the pulse of liberty was beating at its highest, no security against the danger of standing armies was thought necessary beyond a prohibition on their being raised or maintained by the mere authority of the executive . The patriots who brought about that memorable revolution were too temperate, too well-informed, to think of placing any restraint on the legislature's discretion. They understood that a certain number of troops for guards and garrisons was indispensable; that no precise limits could be set to the nation's needs; that a power equal to every possible contingency must exist somewhere in the government; and that, in referring the exercise of that power to the judgment of the legislature, they had arrived at the furthest point of precaution that could be reconciled with the safety of the community.

From that same source, the people of America may be said to have inherited a deeply ingrained impression that standing armies in peacetime pose a danger to liberty. The circumstances of a revolution sharpened public sensitivity on every point connected with the security of popular rights, and in some instances raised the heat of our zeal beyond the level consistent with the sound health of the political community. The attempts of two of the States to restrict legislative authority in the matter of military establishments are among those instances. The principles that had taught us to be wary of the power of a hereditary monarch were, through an injudicious excess, extended to the representatives of the people in their popular assemblies. Even in some of the States where this error was not adopted, we find unnecessary declarations that standing armies ought not to be kept up in time of peace WITHOUT THE CONSENT OF THE LEGISLATURE. I call them unnecessary, because the reason that introduced a similar provision into the English Bill of Rights does not apply to any of the State constitutions. The power of raising armies at all, under those constitutions, cannot by any reading be understood to reside anywhere other than in the legislatures themselves; and it was superfluous, if not absurd, to declare that something should not be done without the consent of a body that alone had the power to do it. Accordingly, in some of these constitutions — and among others, in that of this State of New York, which has been justly celebrated both in Europe and America as one of the best forms of government established in this country — there is total silence on the subject.

It is remarkable that even in the two states that appear to have contemplated an outright ban on military forces in peacetime, the language they chose is cautionary rather than prohibitory. It does not say that standing armies *shall not* be maintained, but that they *ought not* to be maintained, in time of peace. This ambiguity of terms appears to have been the result of a conflict between suspicion and conviction — between the desire to exclude such forces under any circumstances, and the recognition that an absolute exclusion would be unwise and unsafe.

Can it be doubted that such a provision, whenever public circumstances were understood to require a departure from it, would be interpreted by the legislature as a mere advisory warning, and would be made to yield to the necessities or supposed necessities of the State? Let the fact already mentioned regarding Pennsylvania settle the matter. What, then (it may be asked), is the point of such a provision, if it stops operating the moment there is an inclination to disregard it?

Let us examine whether there is any meaningful comparison, in terms of effectiveness, between the provision just mentioned and the one contained in the new Constitution — specifically, the restriction that limits appropriations of money for military purposes to a two-year period. The former, by attempting too much, is designed to accomplish nothing; the latter, by avoiding a reckless extreme and being perfectly compatible with adequate provision for the nation's urgent needs , will have a beneficial and powerful effect.

The legislature of the United States will be OBLIGED, by this provision, at least once every two years, to deliberate on the wisdom of maintaining a military force; to arrive at a fresh resolution on the matter; and to declare their position on it by a formal vote in full view of their constituents. They are not AT LIBERTY to vest in the executive branch permanent funding for the support of an army, even if they were incautious enough to be willing to place in it so improper a trust. Since the spirit of faction , to varying degrees, must be expected to infect all political bodies, there will no doubt be persons in the national legislature willing enough to challenge the conduct and impugn the motives of the majority. The provision for the support of a military force will always be a ready topic for . As often as the question comes before the legislature, the public's attention will be roused and drawn to the subject by the party in opposition; and if the majority should in fact be inclined to exceed proper limits, the community will be warned of the danger and will have an opportunity to take steps to guard against it. Apart from the parties within the national legislature itself, as often as the period of deliberation arrived, the state legislatures — which will always be not only watchful but suspicious and jealous guardians of the rights of citizens against encroachments from the federal government — will constantly keep their attention fixed on the conduct of the national rulers, and will be ready enough, if anything improper appears, to sound the alarm to the people, and to be not only the VOICE, but, if necessary, the ARM of their discontent.

Plots to undermine the liberties of a great community REQUIRE TIME to bring them to the point of execution. An army large enough to seriously threaten those liberties could only be built up through gradual increases — which would presuppose not merely a temporary alliance between the legislature and the executive, but a sustained conspiracy carried on over a stretch of time. Is it probable that such an alliance would exist at all? Is it probable that it would be persisted in, and handed down through all the successive changes in a representative body that biennial elections would naturally produce in both chambers? Is it reasonable to presume that every man, the instant he took his seat in the national Senate or House of Representatives, would become a traitor to his constituents and to his country? Can it be supposed that there would not be found one man perceptive enough to detect so monstrous a conspiracy, or courageous or honest enough to warn his constituents of their danger? If such assumptions can fairly be made, then all delegated authority ought at once to come to an end. The people should resolve to reclaim all the powers they have until now surrendered out of their own hands, and to divide themselves into as many states as there are counties, so that they may be able to manage their own affairs in person.

Even if such assumptions could be reasonably entertained, the concealment of the scheme for any significant length of time would still be impossible. The very act of expanding the army to such a great size during a period of profound peace would itself announce the design. What plausible justification could be offered, in a country so situated, for such vast expansions of the military force? It is impossible that the people could be deceived for long; and the collapse of the scheme, and the ruin of those who devised it, would quickly follow its exposure.

It has been said that the provision limiting the appropriation of money for the support of an army to a two-year period would be useless, because the Executive, once in possession of a force large enough to intimidate the people into submission, would find resources in that very force sufficient to allow him to do without supplies from acts of the legislature. But the question again comes back: on what pretext could he be put in possession of a force of that size in time of peace? If we suppose it to have been created as a result of some domestic insurrection or foreign war, then it becomes a case not covered by the principles of the objection; for the objection is directed against the power of maintaining troops in time of peace. Few people will be so fanciful as to seriously argue that military forces ought not to be raised to put down a rebellion or resist an invasion; and if the defense of the community under such circumstances should make it necessary to have an army so large as to endanger its liberty, this is one of those calamities for which there is neither prevention nor cure. It cannot be guarded against by any possible form of government; it might even result from a simple offensive and defensive alliance, if it should ever be necessary for the confederates or allies to form an army for the common defense.

But this is an evil far less likely to afflict us as a united nation than as a divided one — indeed, it may safely be asserted that it is an evil altogether unlikely to afflict us in the latter situation. It is not easy to conceive of any possibility that dangers so formidable could threaten the whole Union as to demand a force large enough to place our liberties in the slightest jeopardy, especially if we take into account the support to be drawn from the militia, which ought always to be counted upon as a valuable and powerful auxiliary. But in a state of disunion (as has been fully shown elsewhere), the opposite of this supposition would become not only probable, but nearly unavoidable.

publication.raw_dateline: For the Independent Journal. Saturday, December 22, 1788

items.title: The Same Subject Continued (The Idea of Restraining the Legislative Authority in Regard to the Common Defense Considered)
paragraphs: To the People of the State of New York:

IT HAS been urged, in different shapes, that a Constitution of the kind proposed by the convention cannot operate without the aid of a military force to execute its laws. This, however, like most other things that have been alleged on that side, rests on mere general assertion, unsupported by any precise or intelligible designation of the reasons upon which it is founded. As far as I have been able to divine the latent meaning of the objectors, it seems to originate in a presupposition that the people will be disinclined to the exercise of federal authority in any matter of an internal nature. Waiving any exception that might be taken to the inaccuracy or inexplicitness of the distinction between internal and external, let us inquire what ground there is to presuppose that disinclination in the people. Unless we presume at the same time that the powers of the general government will be worse administered than those of the State government, there seems to be no room for the presumption of ill-will, disaffection, or opposition in the people. I believe it may be laid down as a general rule that their confidence in and obedience to a government will commonly be proportioned to the goodness or badness of its administration. It must be admitted that there are exceptions to this rule; but these exceptions depend so entirely on accidental causes, that they cannot be considered as having any relation to the intrinsic merits or demerits of a constitution. These can only be judged of by general principles and maxims.

Various reasons have been suggested, in the course of these papers, to induce a probability that the general government will be better administered than the particular governments; the principal of which reasons are that the extension of the spheres of election will present a greater option, or latitude of choice, to the people; that through the medium of the State legislatures which are select bodies of men, and which are to appoint the members of the national Senate there is reason to expect that this branch will generally be composed with peculiar care and judgment; that these circumstances promise greater knowledge and more extensive information in the national councils, and that they will be less apt to be tainted by the spirit of faction, and more out of the reach of those occasional ill-humors, or temporary prejudices and propensities, which, in smaller societies, frequently contaminate the public councils, beget injustice and oppression of a part of the community, and engender schemes which, though they gratify a momentary inclination or desire, terminate in general distress, dissatisfaction, and disgust. Several additional reasons of considerable force, to fortify that probability, will occur when we come to survey, with a more critical eye, the interior structure of the edifice which we are invited to erect. It will be sufficient here to remark, that until satisfactory reasons can be assigned to justify an opinion, that the federal government is likely to be administered in such a manner as to render it odious or contemptible to the people, there can be no reasonable foundation for the supposition that the laws of the Union will meet with any greater obstruction from them, or will stand in need of any other methods to enforce their execution, than the laws of the particular members.

The hope of impunity is a strong incitement to sedition; the dread of punishment, a proportionably strong discouragement to it. Will not the government of the Union, which, if possessed of a due degree of power, can call to its aid the collective resources of the whole Confederacy, be more likely to repress the FORMER sentiment and to inspire the LATTER, than that of a single State, which can only command the resources within itself? A turbulent faction in a State may easily suppose itself able to contend with the friends to the government in that State; but it can hardly be so infatuated as to imagine itself a match for the combined efforts of the Union. If this reflection be just, there is less danger of resistance from irregular combinations of individuals to the authority of the Confederacy than to that of a single member.

I will, in this place, hazard an observation, which will not be the less just because to some it may appear new; which is, that the more the operations of the national authority are intermingled in the ordinary exercise of government, the more the citizens are accustomed to meet with it in the common occurrences of their political life, the more it is familiarized to their sight and to their feelings, the further it enters into those objects which touch the most sensible chords and put in motion the most active springs of the human heart, the greater will be the probability that it will conciliate the respect and attachment of the community. Man is very much a creature of habit. A thing that rarely strikes his senses will generally have but little influence upon his mind. A government continually at a distance and out of sight can hardly be expected to interest the sensations of the people. The inference is, that the authority of the Union, and the affections of the citizens towards it, will be strengthened, rather than weakened, by its extension to what are called matters of internal concern; and will have less occasion to recur to force, in proportion to the familiarity and comprehensiveness of its agency. The more it circulates through those channels and currents in which the passions of mankind naturally flow, the less will it require the aid of the violent and perilous expedients of compulsion.

One thing, at all events, must be evident, that a government like the one proposed would bid much fairer to avoid the necessity of using force, than that species of league contend for by most of its opponents; the authority of which should only operate upon the States in their political or collective capacities. It has been shown that in such a Confederacy there can be no sanction for the laws but force; that frequent delinquencies in the members are the natural offspring of the very frame of the government; and that as often as these happen, they can only be redressed, if at all, by war and violence.

The plan reported by the convention, by extending the authority of the federal head to the individual citizens of the several States, will enable the government to employ the ordinary magistracy of each, in the execution of its laws. It is easy to perceive that this will tend to destroy, in the common apprehension, all distinction between the sources from which they might proceed; and will give the federal government the same advantage for securing a due obedience to its authority which is enjoyed by the government of each State, in addition to the influence on public opinion which will result from the important consideration of its having power to call to its assistance and support the resources of the whole Union. It merits particular attention in this place, that the laws of the Confederacy, as to the ENUMERATED and LEGITIMATE objects of its jurisdiction, will become the SUPREME LAW of the land; to the observance of which all officers, legislative, executive, and judicial, in each State, will be bound by the sanctity of an oath. Thus the legislatures, courts, and magistrates, of the respective members, will be incorporated into the operations of the national government AS FAR AS ITS JUST AND CONSTITUTIONAL AUTHORITY EXTENDS; and will be rendered auxiliary to the enforcement of its laws.(1) Any man who will pursue, by his own reflections, the consequences of this situation, will perceive that there is good ground to calculate upon a regular and peaceable execution of the laws of the Union, if its powers are administered with a common share of prudence. If we will arbitrarily suppose the contrary, we may deduce any inferences we please from the supposition; for it is certainly possible, by an injudicious exercise of the authorities of the best government that ever was, or ever can be instituted, to provoke and precipitate the people into the wildest excesses. But though the adversaries of the proposed Constitution should presume that the national rulers would be insensible to the motives of public good, or to the obligations of duty, I would still ask them how the interests of ambition, or the views of encroachment, can be promoted by such a conduct?
The sophistry which has been employed to show that this will tend to the destruction of the State governments, will, in its will, in its proper place, be fully detected.

plain_english: To the People of the State of New York:

It has been argued, in various forms, that a Constitution of the kind proposed by the convention cannot function without the aid of military force to execute its laws. This claim, however, like most others that have been made on that side, rests on mere general assertion, unsupported by any precise or intelligible statement of the reasons on which it is based. As far as I have been able to work out the hidden meaning of the objectors, it seems to originate in a presupposition that the people will be unwilling to submit to the exercise of federal authority in any matter of an internal nature. Setting aside any objection that might be raised to the imprecision or vagueness of the distinction between internal and external, let us ask what basis there is for presupposing that unwillingness in the people. Unless we presume at the same time that the powers of the general government will be worse administered than those of the state governments, there seems to be no basis for presuming ill-will, disaffection , or opposition in the people. I believe it can be laid down as a general rule that their confidence in and obedience to a government will ordinarily be proportional to the quality — good or bad — of its administration. It must be admitted that there are exceptions to this rule; but these exceptions depend so entirely on incidental causes that they cannot be regarded as having any relation to the intrinsic merits or defects of a constitution. Those can only be judged by general principles and maxims.

Various reasons have been offered throughout these papers to support the probability that the general government will be better administered than the state governments. The principal reasons are these: that the broader scope of elections will give the people a greater range of choice; that through the medium of the state legislatures — which are select bodies of men and which are responsible for appointing the members of the national Senate — there is reason to expect that this branch will generally be composed with particular care and judgment; that these circumstances promise greater knowledge and more extensive information in the national councils; and that the national councils will be less susceptible to being corrupted by the spirit of faction, and further out of reach of those occasional bad moods , or temporary prejudices and tendencies, which, in smaller societies, frequently corrupt the public councils, breed injustice and oppression of a part of the community, and give rise to schemes which, though they satisfy a momentary inclination or desire, end in general distress, dissatisfaction, and disgust. Several additional reasons of considerable force, which will further support that probability, will emerge when we come to examine, with a more critical eye, the interior structure of the edifice we are invited to erect. It will be sufficient here to note that until satisfactory reasons can be given to justify an opinion that the federal government is likely to be administered in such a manner as to render it odious or contemptible to the people, there can be no reasonable basis for supposing that the laws of the Union will meet with any greater obstruction from them, or will stand in need of any other methods to enforce their execution, than the laws of the individual states.

The hope of escaping punishment is a powerful incentive to rebellion; the fear of punishment, a proportionably powerful deterrent against it. Will not the government of the Union — which, if possessed of an adequate degree of power, can call to its aid the collective resources of the entire Confederacy — be more likely to suppress the FORMER sentiment and to inspire the LATTER, than that of a single State, which can only command the resources within itself? A turbulent faction in a State may easily convince itself that it is capable of contending with the supporters of the government in that State; but it can hardly be so deluded as to imagine itself a match for the combined efforts of the Union. If this reasoning is sound, there is less danger of resistance from irregular combinations of individuals to the authority of the Confederacy than to that of a single member.

I will, at this point, venture an observation that will be no less true for appearing novel to some: the more the operations of the national government are woven into the ordinary exercise of governing, the more citizens grow accustomed to encountering it in the everyday occurrences of their political lives, the more it becomes familiar to their eyes and their feelings, the further it reaches into those matters that touch the most sensitive chords and set in motion the most active impulses of the human heart — the greater will be the probability that it will win the respect and attachment of the community. Human beings are very much creatures of habit. A thing that rarely makes itself felt will generally have little influence on the mind. A government that is continually at a distance and out of sight can hardly be expected to engage the feelings of the people. The conclusion that follows is that the authority of the Union, and the affections of citizens toward it, will be strengthened rather than weakened by its extension to what are called matters of internal concern; and it will have less occasion to resort to force, in proportion to how familiar and wide-ranging its activity becomes. The more it circulates through those channels and currents in which the passions of humankind naturally flow, the less it will require the aid of the violent and dangerous expedients of compulsion.

One thing, at any rate, must be clear: a government like the one proposed would stand a far better chance of avoiding the necessity of using force than that type of league advocated by most of its opponents — a league whose authority would operate only upon the States in their political or collective capacities. It has been shown that in such a confederacy there can be no enforcement mechanism for the laws other than force; that frequent failures to comply on the part of the member states are the natural product of the very structure of the government itself; and that whenever these failures occur, they can only be corrected, if at all, by war and violence.

The plan reported by the convention, by extending the authority of the federal government to the individual citizens of the several states, will enable that government to employ the ordinary of each state in executing its laws. It is easy to see that this will tend to destroy, in the common understanding, all distinction between the sources from which those laws might proceed; and will give the federal government the same advantage in securing proper obedience to its authority that is enjoyed by the government of each individual state — in addition to the influence on public opinion that will result from the important fact that it has the power to call upon and draw from the resources of the entire Union. It deserves particular attention here that the laws of the Confederacy, as to the ENUMERATED and LEGITIMATE objects of its jurisdiction, will become the SUPREME LAW of the land; to the observance of which all officers — legislative, executive, and judicial — in each state will be bound by the sanctity of an oath. Thus the legislatures, courts, and magistrates of the respective member states will be incorporated into the operations of the national government AS FAR AS ITS JUST AND CONSTITUTIONAL AUTHORITY EXTENDS; and will be made auxiliary to the enforcement of its laws.(1) Any person who traces, through their own reasoning, the consequences of this situation will perceive that there is solid ground to expect a regular and peaceful execution of the laws of the Union, provided its powers are administered with a reasonable degree of prudence. If we arbitrarily assume the contrary, we may draw whatever conclusions we please from that assumption; for it is certainly possible, through an injudicious exercise of the authority of the best government that ever was or ever can be established, to provoke and drive the people into the wildest excesses. But even if the opponents of the proposed Constitution were to assume that the national rulers would be indifferent to the motives of public good or to the obligations of duty, I would still ask them how the interests of ambition, or the aims of encroachment, can be advanced by such a course of conduct?
