The great majority of American citizens are, with good reason, convinced that Union is the foundation of their political happiness. Sensible people across all parties now, with few exceptions, agree that it cannot be preserved under the present system, nor without fundamental changes; that new and far-reaching powers ought to be granted to the national government, and that these powers require a different organization of the federal government — a single body being an unsafe custodian of such broad authorities. In conceding all of this, the question of expense must be given up; for it is impossible, with any meaningful degree of safety, to narrow the foundation upon which the system is to rest. The two branches of the legislature are, in the first instance, to consist of only sixty-five persons, which is the same number of which Congress, under the existing Confederation, may be composed. It is true that this number is intended to be increased; but this is to keep pace with the growth of the population and resources of the country. It is evident that a smaller number would, even in the first instance, have been unsafe, and that continuing with the present number would, at a more advanced stage of population, be a very inadequate representation of the people.
Where is this feared growth in expense supposed to come from? One source that has been pointed to is the multiplication of offices under the new government. Let us examine this a little.

It is evident that the principal departments of the administration under the current government are the same ones that will be required under the new one. There is now a Secretary of War, a Secretary of Foreign Affairs, a Secretary for Domestic Affairs, a Board of Treasury consisting of three persons, a Treasurer, assistants, clerks, and so on. These officers are indispensable under any system and will be sufficient under the new as well as the old. As for ambassadors and other ministers and agents in foreign countries, the proposed Constitution can make no other difference than to make their standing, in the countries where they reside, more respectable, and their services more useful. As for persons to be employed in the collection of revenues, it is unquestionably true that these will form a very considerable addition to the number of federal officers; but it does not follow from this that it will result in an increase of public expense. In most cases it will be nothing more than an exchange of state for national officers. In the collection of all duties, for instance, the persons employed will be entirely of the latter description. The individual states will have no need of any such officers for this purpose. What difference can it make in terms of expense whether you pay customs officers appointed by the state or by the United States? There is no good reason to suppose that either the number or the salaries of the latter will be greater than those of the former.

Where, then, are we to look for those additional items of expense that are supposed to swell the total to the enormous size we have been told to expect? The chief item that comes to mind concerns the compensation of the judges of the United States. I do not include the President, because there is currently a president of Congress whose expenses may not fall far short of — if at all below — those that will be incurred for the President of the United States. The compensation of the judges will clearly be an extra expense, but to what extent will depend on the particular plan that may be adopted on this matter. On no reasonable plan, however, can it amount to a sum of any material consequence.

Now let us consider what there is to offset any extra expense that may come with establishing the proposed government. The first thing that presents itself is that a large part of the business that currently keeps Congress in session throughout the year will be handled by the President. Even the conduct of foreign negotiations will naturally fall to him, according to general principles worked out with the Senate and subject to their final approval. It follows, then, that a portion of the year will be sufficient for the sessions of both the Senate and the House of Representatives — roughly a fourth of the year for the latter and a third, or perhaps half, for the former. The additional business of treaties and appointments may account for this additional demand on the Senate's time. From this we can infer that, until the House of Representatives is enlarged well beyond its current size, there will be a considerable savings in expense owing to the difference between the continuous session of the present Congress and the limited session of the future one.

But there is another circumstance of great importance from the standpoint of economy. The business of the United States has until now occupied the state legislatures as well as Congress. Congress has made requisitions which the state legislatures have had to provide for. As a result, the sessions of the state legislatures have been prolonged well beyond what was necessary for carrying out the purely local business of the states. More than half their time has frequently been consumed by matters relating to the United States. Now, the members who make up the legislatures of the several states number two thousand and upward — a number that has until now performed what, under the new system, will be done in the first instance by sixty-five persons, and probably at no future point by more than a fourth or fifth of that number. Congress under the proposed government will conduct all the business of the United States on its own, without the involvement of the state legislatures, which from that point forward will need only to attend to the affairs of their particular states, and will not need to remain in session anywhere near as long as they have done up to now. This reduction in the time spent in session by the state legislatures will be clear gain, and will by itself constitute an item of savings that may be considered equivalent to any additional expenses that the adoption of the new system might occasion.

What follows from these observations is that the sources of additional expense arising from the establishment of the proposed Constitution are far fewer than may have been supposed; that they are offset by considerable areas of savings; and that while it remains an open question on which side the balance will tip, one thing is certain: a government any less expensive would be unequal to the purposes of the Union.

publication.raw_dateline: From McLEAN's Edition, New York. Wednesday, May 28, 1788

items.title: Concluding Remarks
paragraphs: To the People of the State of New York:

ACCORDING to the formal division of the subject of these papers, announced in my first number, there would appear still to remain for discussion two points: "the analogy of the proposed government to your own State constitution," and "the additional security which its adoption will afford to republican government, to liberty, and to property." But these heads have been so fully anticipated and exhausted in the progress of the work, that it would now scarcely be possible to do any thing more than repeat, in a more dilated form, what has been heretofore said, which the advanced stage of the question, and the time already spent upon it, conspire to forbid.

It is remarkable, that the resemblance of the plan of the convention to the act which organizes the government of this State holds, not less with regard to many of the supposed defects, than to the real excellences of the former. Among the pretended defects are the re-eligibility of the Executive, the want of a council, the omission of a formal bill of rights, the omission of a provision respecting the liberty of the press. These and several others which have been noted in the course of our inquiries are as much chargeable on the existing constitution of this State, as on the one proposed for the Union; and a man must have slender pretensions to consistency, who can rail at the latter for imperfections which he finds no difficulty in excusing in the former. Nor indeed can there be a better proof of the insincerity and affectation of some of the zealous adversaries of the plan of the convention among us, who profess to be the devoted admirers of the government under which they live, than the fury with which they have attacked that plan, for matters in regard to which our own constitution is equally or perhaps more vulnerable.

The additional securities to republican government, to liberty and to property, to be derived from the adoption of the plan under consideration, consist chiefly in the restraints which the preservation of the Union will impose on local factions and insurrections, and on the ambition of powerful individuals in single States, who may acquire credit and influence enough, from leaders and favorites, to become the despots of the people; in the diminution of the opportunities to foreign intrigue, which the dissolution of the Confederacy would invite and facilitate; in the prevention of extensive military establishments, which could not fail to grow out of wars between the States in a disunited situation; in the express guaranty of a republican form of government to each; in the absolute and universal exclusion of titles of nobility; and in the precautions against the repetition of those practices on the part of the State governments which have undermined the foundations of property and credit, have planted mutual distrust in the breasts of all classes of citizens, and have occasioned an almost universal prostration of morals.

Thus have I, fellow-citizens, executed the task I had assigned to myself; with what success, your conduct must determine. I trust at least you will admit that I have not failed in the assurance I gave you respecting the spirit with which my endeavors should be conducted. I have addressed myself purely to your judgments, and have studiously avoided those asperities which are too apt to disgrace political disputants of all parties, and which have been not a little provoked by the language and conduct of the opponents of the Constitution. The charge of a conspiracy against the liberties of the people, which has been indiscriminately brought against the advocates of the plan, has something in it too wanton and too malignant, not to excite the indignation of every man who feels in his own bosom a refutation of the calumny. The perpetual changes which have been rung upon the wealthy, the well-born, and the great, have been such as to inspire the disgust of all sensible men. And the unwarrantable concealments and misrepresentations which have been in various ways practiced to keep the truth from the public eye, have been of a nature to demand the reprobation of all honest men. It is not impossible that these circumstances may have occasionally betrayed me into intemperances of expression which I did not intend; it is certain that I have frequently felt a struggle between sensibility and moderation; and if the former has in some instances prevailed, it must be my excuse that it has been neither often nor much.

Let us now pause and ask ourselves whether, in the course of these papers, the proposed Constitution has not been satisfactorily vindicated from the aspersions thrown upon it; and whether it has not been shown to be worthy of the public approbation, and necessary to the public safety and prosperity. Every man is bound to answer these questions to himself, according to the best of his conscience and understanding, and to act agreeably to the genuine and sober dictates of his judgment. This is a duty from which nothing can give him a dispensation. 'T is one that he is called upon, nay, constrained by all the obligations that form the bands of society, to discharge sincerely and honestly. No partial motive, no particular interest, no pride of opinion, no temporary passion or prejudice, will justify to himself, to his country, or to his posterity, an improper election of the part he is to act. Let him beware of an obstinate adherence to party; let him reflect that the object upon which he is to decide is not a particular interest of the community, but the very existence of the nation; and let him remember that a majority of America has already given its sanction to the plan which he is to approve or reject.

I shall not dissemble that I feel an entire confidence in the arguments which recommend the proposed system to your adoption, and that I am unable to discern any real force in those by which it has been opposed. I am persuaded that it is the best which our political situation, habits, and opinions will admit, and superior to any the revolution has produced.

Concessions on the part of the friends of the plan, that it has not a claim to absolute perfection, have afforded matter of no small triumph to its enemies. "Why," say they, "should we adopt an imperfect thing? Why not amend it and make it perfect before it is irrevocably established?" This may be plausible enough, but it is only plausible. In the first place I remark, that the extent of these concessions has been greatly exaggerated. They have been stated as amounting to an admission that the plan is radically defective, and that without material alterations the rights and the interests of the community cannot be safely confided to it. This, as far as I have understood the meaning of those who make the concessions, is an entire perversion of their sense. No advocate of the measure can be found, who will not declare as his sentiment, that the system, though it may not be perfect in every part, is, upon the whole, a good one; is the best that the present views and circumstances of the country will permit; and is such an one as promises every species of security which a reasonable people can desire.

I answer in the next place, that I should esteem it the extreme of imprudence to prolong the precarious state of our national affairs, and to expose the Union to the jeopardy of successive experiments, in the chimerical pursuit of a perfect plan. I never expect to see a perfect work from imperfect man. The result of the deliberations of all collective bodies must necessarily be a compound, as well of the errors and prejudices, as of the good sense and wisdom, of the individuals of whom they are composed. The compacts which are to embrace thirteen distinct States in a common bond of amity and union, must as necessarily be a compromise of as many dissimilar interests and inclinations. How can perfection spring from such materials?

The reasons assigned in an excellent little pamphlet lately published in this city,(1) are unanswerable to show the utter improbability of assembling a new convention, under circumstances in any degree so favorable to a happy issue, as those in which the late convention met, deliberated, and concluded. I will not repeat the arguments there used, as I presume the production itself has had an extensive circulation. It is certainly well worthy the perusal of every friend to his country. There is, however, one point of light in which the subject of amendments still remains to be considered, and in which it has not yet been exhibited to public view. I cannot resolve to conclude without first taking a survey of it in this aspect.

It appears to me susceptible of absolute demonstration, that it will be far more easy to obtain subsequent than previous amendments to the Constitution. The moment an alteration is made in the present plan, it becomes, to the purpose of adoption, a new one, and must undergo a new decision of each State. To its complete establishment throughout the Union, it will therefore require the concurrence of thirteen States. If, on the contrary, the Constitution proposed should once be ratified by all the States as it stands, alterations in it may at any time be effected by nine States. Here, then, the chances are as thirteen to nine(2) in favor of subsequent amendment, rather than of the original adoption of an entire system.

This is not all. Every Constitution for the United States must inevitably consist of a great variety of particulars, in which thirteen independent States are to be accommodated in their interests or opinions of interest. We may of course expect to see, in any body of men charged with its original formation, very different combinations of the parts upon different points. Many of those who form a majority on one question, may become the minority on a second, and an association dissimilar to either may constitute the majority on a third. Hence the necessity of moulding and arranging all the particulars which are to compose the whole, in such a manner as to satisfy all the parties to the compact; and hence, also, an immense multiplication of difficulties and casualties in obtaining the collective assent to a final act. The degree of that multiplication must evidently be in a ratio to the number of particulars and the number of parties.

But every amendment to the Constitution, if once established, would be a single proposition, and might be brought forward singly. There would then be no necessity for management or compromise, in relation to any other point--no giving nor taking. The will of the requisite number would at once bring the matter to a decisive issue. And consequently, whenever nine, or rather ten States, were united in the desire of a particular amendment, that amendment must infallibly take place. There can, therefore, be no comparison between the facility of affecting an amendment, and that of establishing in the first instance a complete Constitution.

In opposition to the probability of subsequent amendments, it has been urged that the persons delegated to the administration of the national government will always be disinclined to yield up any portion of the authority of which they were once possessed. For my own part I acknowledge a thorough conviction that any amendments which may, upon mature consideration, be thought useful, will be applicable to the organization of the government, not to the mass of its powers; and on this account alone, I think there is no weight in the observation just stated. I also think there is little weight in it on another account. The intrinsic difficulty of governing THIRTEEN STATES at any rate, independent of calculations upon an ordinary degree of public spirit and integrity, will, in my opinion constantly impose on the national rulers the necessity of a spirit of accommodation to the reasonable expectations of their constituents. But there is yet a further consideration, which proves beyond the possibility of a doubt, that the observation is futile. It is this that the national rulers, whenever nine States concur, will have no option upon the subject. By the fifth article of the plan, the Congress will be obliged "on the application of the legislatures of two thirds of the States (which at present amount to nine), to call a convention for proposing amendments, which shall be valid, to all intents and purposes, as part of the Constitution, when ratified by the legislatures of three fourths of the States, or by conventions in three fourths thereof." The words of this article are peremptory. The Congress "shall call a convention." Nothing in this particular is left to the discretion of that body. And of consequence, all the declamation about the disinclination to a change vanishes in air. Nor however difficult it may be supposed to unite two thirds or three fourths of the State legislatures, in amendments which may affect local interests, can there be any room to apprehend any such difficulty in a union on points which are merely relative to the general liberty or security of the people. We may safely rely on the disposition of the State legislatures to erect barriers against the encroachments of the national authority.

If the foregoing argument is a fallacy, certain it is that I am myself deceived by it, for it is, in my conception, one of those rare instances in which a political truth can be brought to the test of a mathematical demonstration. Those who see the matter in the same light with me, however zealous they may be for amendments, must agree in the propriety of a previous adoption, as the most direct road to their own object.

The zeal for attempts to amend, prior to the establishment of the Constitution, must abate in every man who is ready to accede to the truth of the following observations of a writer equally solid and ingenious: "To balance a large state or society (says he), whether monarchical or republican, on general laws, is a work of so great difficulty, that no human genius, however comprehensive, is able, by the mere dint of reason and reflection, to effect it. The judgments of many must unite in the work; EXPERIENCE must guide their labor; TIME must bring it to perfection, and the FEELING of inconveniences must correct the mistakes which they inevitably fall into in their first trials and experiments."(3) These judicious reflections contain a lesson of moderation to all the sincere lovers of the Union, and ought to put them upon their guard against hazarding anarchy, civil war, a perpetual alienation of the States from each other, and perhaps the military despotism of a victorious demagogue, in the pursuit of what they are not likely to obtain, but from TIME and EXPERIENCE. It may be in me a defect of political fortitude, but I acknowledge that I cannot entertain an equal tranquillity with those who affect to treat the dangers of a longer continuance in our present situation as imaginary. A NATION, without a NATIONAL GOVERNMENT, is, in my view, an awful spectacle. The establishment of a Constitution, in time of profound peace, by the voluntary consent of a whole people, is a PRODIGY, to the completion of which I look forward with trembling anxiety. I can reconcile it to no rules of prudence to let go the hold we now have, in so arduous an enterprise, upon seven out of the thirteen States, and after having passed over so considerable a part of the ground, to recommence the course. I dread the more the consequences of new attempts, because I know that POWERFUL INDIVIDUALS, in this and in other States, are enemies to a general national government in every possible shape.
Entitled "An Address to the People of the State of New York."
It may rather be said TEN, for though two thirds may set on foot the measure, three fourths must ratify.
Hume's Essays, Vol. I, p. 128: "The Rise of Arts and Sciences."

plain_english: To the People of the State of New York:

According to the formal division of the subject of these papers, announced in my first number, there would appear still to remain for discussion two points: "the analogy of the proposed government to your own State constitution," and "the additional security which its adoption will afford to republican government, to liberty, and to property." But these topics have been so fully anticipated and exhausted in the course of this work that it would now scarcely be possible to do anything more than repeat, in a more drawn-out form, what has already been said — which the advanced stage of the question, and the time already spent upon it, both argue against.

It is remarkable that the resemblance of the convention's plan to the act that establishes the government of this State holds not only with respect to many of the genuine strengths of the former, but equally with respect to many of its supposed defects. Among the alleged defects are the re-eligibility of the Executive, the absence of a council, the omission of a formal bill of rights, and the omission of a provision protecting the freedom of the press. These and several others that have been noted in the course of our inquiry are just as fairly chargeable against the existing constitution of this State as against the one proposed for the Union; and a man must have who can rail at the latter for imperfections he finds no difficulty excusing in the former. Nor indeed can there be better proof of the insincerity and affectation of some of the zealous opponents of the convention's plan among us — who profess to be devoted admirers of the government under which they live — than the fury with which they have attacked that plan for matters in which our own constitution is equally, or perhaps more, vulnerable.

The additional protections that republican government, liberty, and property would gain from adopting the plan under consideration consist chiefly in: the restraints that preserving the Union would place on local factions and insurrections, and on the ambitions of powerful individuals within individual states who might accumulate enough credit and influence — through followers and favorites — to become the despots of the people; the reduction of opportunities for foreign interference, which the dissolution of the Confederacy would invite and enable; the prevention of large standing military establishments, which would inevitably grow out of wars between the states in a disunited condition; the explicit guarantee of a republican form of government to each state; the absolute and universal exclusion of titles of nobility; and the precautions against a repetition of those practices by state governments that have undermined the foundations of property and credit, planted mutual distrust in the hearts of all classes of citizens, and brought about an almost universal collapse of morals.

And so, fellow citizens, I have completed the task I set for myself; how well I have done so, your response must determine. I trust you will at least acknowledge that I have not fallen short of the assurance I gave you regarding the spirit in which my efforts would be carried out. I have addressed myself purely to your judgment, and have carefully avoided those harsh attacks that are all too likely to disgrace political disputants of every party, and which were provoked, to no small degree, by the language and conduct of the Constitution's opponents. The charge of conspiracy against the liberties of the people — leveled indiscriminately against every advocate of the plan — has something in it too wanton and too malignant not to excite the indignation of every man who finds in his own conscience a refutation of the slander. The endless variations that have been played upon the themes of the wealthy, the well-born, and the great have been of a kind to inspire disgust in all sensible men. And the unjustifiable concealments and misrepresentations that have been practiced in various ways to keep the truth from the public eye have been of a nature to warrant the condemnation of all honest men. It is not impossible that these circumstances may have occasionally led me into intemperate expressions I did not intend; it is certain that I have frequently felt a struggle between feeling and restraint; and if the former has in some instances prevailed, my excuse must be that it happened neither often nor to a great degree.

Let us pause now and ask ourselves whether, in the course of these papers, the proposed Constitution has not been satisfactorily defended against the attacks thrown upon it; and whether it has not been shown to be worthy of public approval, and necessary to the public safety and prosperity. Every man is bound to answer these questions for himself, according to the best of his conscience and understanding, and to act in accordance with the genuine and sober dictates of his judgment. This is a duty from which nothing can release him. It is one that he is called upon — indeed, compelled by all the obligations that form the bonds of society — to discharge sincerely and honestly. No partial motive, no particular interest, no pride of opinion, no temporary passion or prejudice, will justify to himself, to his country, or to his posterity, an improper choice of the part he is to play. Let him beware of an obstinate adherence to party; let him reflect that the object upon which he is to decide is not a particular interest of the community, but the very existence of the nation; and let him remember that a majority of America has already given its sanction to the plan which he is to approve or reject.

I will not pretend that I feel anything other than complete confidence in the arguments that recommend the proposed system to your adoption, or that I can identify any genuine force in the arguments by which it has been opposed. I am convinced that it is the best system our political situation, habits, and opinions will allow, and superior to any that the Revolution has produced.

Concessions on the part of supporters of the plan — that it cannot claim absolute perfection — have given its opponents no small occasion for triumph. "Why," they say, "should we adopt an imperfect thing? Why not amend it and make it perfect before it is irrevocably established?" This may be plausible enough, but it is only plausible. In the first place, I note that the extent of these concessions has been greatly exaggerated. They have been characterized as amounting to an admission that the plan is radically defective, and that without substantial alterations the rights and interests of the community cannot be safely entrusted to it. This, as far as I have understood the meaning of those who make the concessions, is an entire distortion of their intent. No advocate of the measure can be found who will not declare as his view that the system, though it may not be perfect in every part, is on the whole a good one; is the best that the country's current circumstances and outlook will allow; and is one that promises every kind of security that a reasonable people can desire.

My second answer is this: I would consider it the height of recklessness to prolong the unstable condition of our national affairs, and to expose the Union to the danger of one experiment after another, in the I never expect to see a perfect work come from imperfect man. The outcome of the deliberations of any collective body must necessarily be a compound of both the errors and prejudices and the good sense and wisdom of the individuals who compose it. The agreements that are to bind thirteen distinct states in a common bond of friendship and union must just as necessarily be a compromise among as many differing interests and inclinations. How can perfection spring from such materials?

The arguments laid out in an excellent short pamphlet recently published in this city (1) are conclusive in demonstrating the utter improbability of assembling a new convention under circumstances anywhere near as favorable to a successful outcome as those in which the recent convention met, deliberated, and reached its conclusions. I will not repeat the arguments made there, since I assume the pamphlet itself has already circulated widely. It is certainly well worth the attention of every friend to his country. There is, however, one angle from which the subject of amendments still remains to be considered — one that has not yet been presented to public view. I cannot bring myself to conclude without first examining it from that perspective.

It seems to me capable of absolute demonstration that it will be far easier to obtain amendments to the Constitution after ratification than before it. The moment any alteration is made to the current plan, it becomes, for the purpose of adoption, an entirely new plan, and must undergo a new decision by each State. To achieve its complete establishment throughout the Union, it would therefore require the agreement of all thirteen States. If, on the contrary, the proposed Constitution were once ratified by all the States as it stands, alterations to it may at any time be made by nine States. Here, then, the odds are thirteen to nine(2) in favor of subsequent amendment, rather than of originally adopting an entirely revised system.

This is not all. Every Constitution for the United States must inevitably consist of a great variety of specific provisions, in which thirteen independent States must be accommodated in their interests or their perceived interests. We may therefore expect to see, in any body of men charged with drafting it from scratch, very different coalitions forming around different points. Many of those who form a majority on one question may become the minority on a second, and a coalition unlike either may constitute the majority on a third. Hence the necessity of shaping and arranging all the specific provisions that are to compose the whole in such a manner as to satisfy all the parties to the compact; and hence, also, an immense multiplication of difficulties and contingencies in obtaining the collective assent to a final act. The degree of that multiplication must evidently be proportional to the number of provisions and the number of parties.

But every amendment to the Constitution, once established, would be a single proposition and could be brought forward on its own. There would then be no need for negotiation or compromise in relation to any other point — no trading or conceding. The will of the required number would immediately bring the matter to a decisive resolution. And consequently, whenever nine, or rather ten, states were united in the desire for a particular amendment, that amendment would infallibly take place. There can, therefore, be no comparison between the ease of achieving an amendment and that of establishing, in the first instance, a complete Constitution.

Against the likelihood of subsequent amendments, it has been argued that the people entrusted with running the national government will always be reluctant to give up any portion of the authority they have once acquired. For my own part, I acknowledge a thorough conviction that any amendments which may, upon careful consideration, be thought useful will apply to the organization of the government — not to the bulk of its powers — and on this account alone I think there is no weight in the objection just stated. I also think there is little weight in it on another account. The inherent difficulty of governing THIRTEEN STATES under any circumstances, independent of calculations based on an ordinary degree of public spirit and integrity, will, in my opinion, constantly impose on the national rulers the necessity of a spirit of accommodation to the reasonable expectations of their constituents. But there is yet a further consideration, which proves beyond any possibility of doubt that the objection is worthless. It is this: that the national rulers, whenever nine States agree, will have no option on the subject. By the fifth article of the plan, Congress will be obliged "on the application of the legislatures of two thirds of the States (which at present amount to nine), to call a convention for proposing amendments, which shall be valid, to all intents and purposes, as part of the Constitution, when ratified by the legislatures of three fourths of the States, or by conventions in three fourths thereof." The words of this article are The Congress "shall call a convention." Nothing in this particular is left to the discretion of that body. And consequently, all the Nor, however difficult it may be supposed to unite two thirds or three fourths of the State legislatures in amendments that may affect local interests, can there be any room to fear any such difficulty in uniting on points that are purely about the general liberty or security of the people. We may safely rely on the disposition of the State legislatures to erect barriers against the encroachments of the national authority.

If the foregoing argument is a fallacy, then I am certainly deceiving myself by it — for it is, in my view, one of those rare instances in which a political truth can be subjected to the test of a mathematical demonstration. Those who see the matter as I do, however zealous they may be for amendments, must agree on the appropriateness of first adopting the Constitution, as the most direct path to their own goal.

The eagerness to pursue amendments before the Constitution is established should diminish in every person who is willing to accept the truth of the following observations by a writer who is equally rigorous and inventive: "To balance a large state or society (says he), whether monarchical or republican, on general laws, is a work of so great difficulty, that no human genius, however comprehensive, is able, by the mere dint of reason and reflection, to effect it. The judgments of many must unite in the work; EXPERIENCE must guide their labor; TIME must bring it to perfection, and the FEELING of inconveniences must correct the mistakes which they inevitably fall into in their first trials and experiments."(3) These sound reflections contain a lesson of moderation for all sincere lovers of the Union, and ought to put them on guard against risking anarchy, civil war, a permanent estrangement of the States from one another, and perhaps the military despotism of a victorious demagogue, in the pursuit of what they are unlikely to obtain except through TIME and EXPERIENCE. It may be a defect of political courage in me, but I acknowledge that I cannot maintain the same calm as those who claim to treat the dangers of a longer continuation in our present situation as imaginary. A NATION, without a NATIONAL GOVERNMENT, is, in my view, an awful spectacle. The establishment of a Constitution, in a time of profound peace, by the voluntary consent of a whole people, is a PRODIGY, to the completion of which I look forward with trembling anxiety. I cannot reconcile it with any rule of prudence to release the hold we now have, in so difficult an undertaking, upon seven of the thirteen States, and after having already covered so considerable a portion of the ground, to start the course over again. I dread the consequences of new attempts all the more because I know that POWERFUL INDIVIDUALS, in this and in other States, are enemies to a general national government in every conceivable form.

publication.raw_dateline: From MCLEAN's Edition, New York. Wednesday, May 28, 1788
